summary: Spolsky responds to Jakob Nielsen's claim that Flash is '99% bad,' partially agreeing that Flash reduces usability but pushing back on Nielsen's absolutist stance. He argues that usability, while important, is not always the top priority and shouldn't be treated as the only metric that matters. Using a vivid analogy about singles bars, he illustrates that optimizing purely for usability can strip away the qualities that actually attract people.
key_points: Flash does reduce usability, confirming Nielsen's technical criticism
Usability is important but not the supreme design value that trumps all others
Optimizing purely for usability can produce sterile experiences that nobody wants
Appeal, atmosphere, and fun are legitimate design goals that sometimes conflict with usability
money_quotes: Every time I read Jakob Nielsen, I get this feeling that he really doesn't appreciate that usability is not the most important thing on earth.
Usability is simply not everyone's number one priority, nor should it be.
You get the feeling that if Mr Nielsen designed a singles bar, it would be well lit, clean, with giant menus printed in Arial 14 point, and you'd never have to wait to get a drink.
But nobody would go there, they would all be at Coyote Ugly Saloon pouring beer on each other.
key_insight: Usability is necessary but not sufficient — designing purely for usability while ignoring appeal and experience produces products nobody actually wants to use.
